message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 10
Recent Green phase: Added `moonstone: 25` to BASE_PREMIUMS.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green.

Note: We now know that `isRuneBlock` is going to need to extend to ANY component type forming a block of 3. The next test is "3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G (two separate blocks)" — so the predicate will need to generalize to "is this a homogenous block of any component type".

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- red: TDD Red Phase
- green: TDD Green Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
PRE measurements:
- ESLint smells: 0
- 10 tests passing

Now let me compute APP mass and McCabe for each function:

**Naming Evaluation**:
- `isRuneBlock`: Currently checks "is this a block of 3 runes". The next test ("3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G two separate blocks") will require this to generalize. The name is misleading — it should be about ANY block. **Recommendation: rename to something like `isComponentBlock` or `formsBlock`** to reveal the general intent.
- `sumOfBasePremiums`: Clear, fits.
- `basePremiumFor`: Clear, fits.
- `quotePremium`: Clear, fits.
- `processScenario`: Clear, fits.

**APP Mass — PRE**:

`isRuneBlock`:
- Constants: 1 (`"rune"`)
- Bindings: 3 (items, item parameter, COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE access — let me re-count: `items`, `item`, plus access to `COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE` and `items.length`/`items.every`/`item.type`)
- Bindings: 2 (items, item)
- Invocations: 2 (`.every`, comparisons `===` x2)
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: 1 (`.every`)
- Mass: 1·1 + 2·1 + 2·2 + 0·4 + 1·5 + 0·6 = 12

`sumOfBasePremiums`:
- Constants: 1 (`0`)
- Bindings: 3 (items, sum, item)
- Invocations: 2 (`reduce`, `+`)
- Loops: 1 (reduce)
- Mass: 1 + 3 + 4 + 5 = 13

`basePremiumFor`:
- Bindings: 1 (items)
- Invocations: 2 (`isRuneBlock`, `sumOfBasePremiums`)
- Conditionals: 1 (ternary)
- Mass: 1 + 4 + 4 = 9

`quotePremium`:
- Constants: 0
- Bindings: 2 (items, base)
- Invocations: ~5 (basePremiumFor, ceil, + x2, *)
- Mass: 2 + 10 = 12

**McCabe — PRE**:
- `isRuneBlock`: 1 (no branching; `.every` is a method call, no McCabe-relevant branch — wait, `&&` adds 1) → 1 + 1 (the `&&`) = 2
- `sumOfBasePremiums`: 1
- `basePremiumFor`: 1 + 1 (ternary) = 2
- `quotePremium`: 1
- `processScenario`: 1

Worst McCabe: `isRuneBlock` and `basePremiumFor` tied at 2.

**Naming is the priority refactoring**: The name `isRuneBlock` will become wrong as soon as the next test arrives. Since the rule is "Reveals Intent" (Rule 2) takes priority and we know the function needs to generalize, let me rename it now to reflect the general predicate. The function already checks "all items are of the same type" via `every`, just with the type hardcoded to `"rune"`.

Actually, the truly minimal rename that matches **current behavior** (block of 3 runes specifically) but is more general in name would be misleading. Let me reconsider.

The current function literally checks: length is 3 AND all are runes. The simplest naming change that reveals intent for the next test: I can rename to `isComponentBlock` and parameterize by type... but that changes behavior.

Better approach: stay strict to current tests but use a more honest name. The function checks "all items are runes and there are exactly 3". I could rename to `formsRuneBlock` which is clearer. But the comment about the future generalization is informative — the question is whether to make a structural refactoring now or just rename.

Let me make ONE focused change: rename `isRuneBlock` to `formsHomogenousBlock` and refactor it to take a generalized predicate that works on any homogeneous-type block. This still produces the same result for the current 10 tests (since "all of same type" works for runes too), but generalizes the function name without changing what it does behaviorally for current tests.

Wait — if I generalize the function to "all same type and length 3", will current tests still pass? Yes:
- 3 runes: all same type, length 3 → true → block premium 60 ✓
- 4 runes: length 4 ≠ 3 → false ✓
- 2 runes + 1 moonstone: not all same type → false → sum = 25+25+25 = 75 ✓

So the generalization is safe AND prepares for the next test. Let me do this refactoring.

content.text:
All 10 tests still pass and ESLint is still clean. Task tracking isn't really applicable for this short refactoring session.

**POST measurements**:

`formsComponentBlock` (renamed from `isRuneBlock`):
- Constants: 0 (the `0` index, no string literal anymore)
- Wait: `items[0]` — the `0` is a numeric constant: 1
- Bindings: 3 (items, item, items[0].type access via items[0])
- Invocations: 2 (.every, accessing .type, comparisons)
- Loops: 1 (.every)
- Mass: 1 + 3 + 4 + 5 = 13 (was 12; +1 from extra binding/access)

McCabe for `formsComponentBlock`: still 2 (one `&&`, no other branches added).

Other functions unchanged.

**Pre/Post Deltas**:

| Metric | PRE | POST | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| ESLint smells | 0 | 0 | unchanged |
| Tests passing | 10/10 | 10/10 | unchanged |
| `isRuneBlock`/`formsComponentBlock` APP mass | 12 | 13 | +1 |
| `formsComponentBlock` McCabe | 2 | 2 | unchanged |
| Naming reveals intent | "isRuneBlock" hardcoded a type | "formsComponentBlock" matches generalized purpose | improved |

**Refactoring Applied**: Renamed `isRuneBlock` to `formsComponentBlock` and generalized the predicate from "every item is rune" to "every item matches items[0].type". This applies Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) which is higher priority than APP mass (the +1 mass increase is the cost of clearer, more general intent — the hardcoded `"rune"` literal misrepresented the future shape of the function).

**Verification that current tests still pass**:
- 3 runes: all same type, length 3 → block premium 60 ✓
- 4 runes: length ≠ 3 → no block, sum-based pricing ✓
- 2 runes + 1 moonstone: items[0] is rune, items[2] is moonstone — not all match → no block, sum 75 ✓

**Bonus**: the next test ("3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G, two separate blocks") will still require partitioning logic, but `formsComponentBlock` will now correctly identify a 3-moonstone block when applied to a homogeneous moonstone slice. The predicate is ready; only the partitioning remains for the next Green phase.

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-27_14-28-32_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

Refactoring Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Renamed and generalized `isRuneBlock` → `formsComponentBlock` (predicate now compares types via `items[0].type` rather than hardcoded `"rune"`)
- **Smell delta**: 0 → 0
- **Cognitive delta** (target function): n/a (no ESLint cognitive complexity flags pre or post)
- **APP mass delta** (target function): 12 → 13 (+1, accepted in service of Rule 2)
- **McCabe delta** (target function): 2 → 2
- **Tests**: 10/10 passing
